title: GDPR Security Testing Evidence Aggregation (Pen Test / Red Team)
exclusions: Vulnerabilities formally accepted through the risk management governance process with documented compensating controls and executive sign-off; systems scheduled for decommissioning within 90 days.
evidence: Security testing evidence pack showing penetration test dates, scope, critical finding counts, remediation status, and executive sign-off on residual risk acceptance.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.32(1)(d) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.32(1)(d) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.24: GDPR Security Testing Evidence Aggregation (Pen Test / Red Team).
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.24' running on gdpr_security_test_register.csv, index=itsm (remediation tickets), tenable:vuln (scan completion), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Log a penetration test report with 3 critical findings and no corresponding remediation tickets created within 14 days; verify the evidence aggregation search identifies the unresolved critical findings.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Log a penetration test report with all findings having corresponding remediation tickets created within 7 days and tracked to closure; verify the aggregation reports full evidence coverage.

app: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060)
spl:
| inputlookup gdpr_security_test_register.csv
| eval test_date_epoch=strptime(last_test_date,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval days_since=round((now()-test_date_epoch)/86400,0)
| eval cadence_days=case(test_type="external_pen_test", 365, test_type="internal_va", 90, 1=1, 180)
| eval status=case(days_since > cadence_days, "OVERDUE", days_since > cadence_days*0.8, "DUE_SOON", 1=1, "CURRENT")
| lookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv system_name OUTPUT processing_activity
| where status!="CURRENT"
| table system_name, processing_activity, test_type, last_test_date, days_since, cadence_days, status
| sort status, -days_since
description: Article 32(1)(d) requires a process for regularly testing, assessing, and evaluating the effectiveness of security measures. This use case centralises penetration test, red-team, and vulnerability-assessment completion dates, scope coverage against personal-data systems, and remediation SLAs — proving the testing programme exists and covers GDPR-relevant assets.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Populate `gdpr_security_test_register.csv` with last test dates and types per in-scope system; (2) join to ROPA for processing-activity context; (3) alert when tests exceed policy cadence; (4) link ServiceNow remediation tickets for critical findings; (5) export annual effectiveness summary for Art. 32 evidence. Indexes required: `index=itsm`.
visualization: Table (overdue tests), Timeline (tests by quarter), Single value (systems past cadence), Bar chart (coverage by business unit). Key fields: `cadence_days`, `days_since`, `last_test_date`, `processing_activity`, `status`, `system_name`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Scheduled penetration test activities** — Authorised penetration testing generates security events that should correlate with the approved test window in the change management system; suppress alerts during documented testing periods.

2. **Red team exercise coordination** — Sanctioned red team activities may trigger security controls and generate evidence that appears as genuine attacks; cross-reference with the red team exercise register.

3. **Vulnerability scanner noise** — Automated vulnerability scanners produce findings that require triage; new scan results may appear as security gaps before the remediation cycle classifies them.

4. **Decommissioned system legacy findings** — Findings against systems scheduled for decommissioning within the accepted risk window may persist in reports until the system is retired.

5. **Accepted risk items** — Vulnerabilities formally accepted through the risk management process with documented compensating controls appear as open findings in testing evidence but have governance approval.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_ropa_register.csv`, `gdpr_security_test_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_ropa_register.csv','gdpr_security_test_register.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.32(1)(d)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.32(1)(d) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.24: GDPR Security Testing Evidence Aggregation (Pen Test / Red Team)." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Populate `gdpr_security_test_register.csv` with last test dates and types per in-scope system. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 2:** join to ROPA for processing-activity context. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert when tests exceed policy cadence. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** link ServiceNow remediation tickets for critical findings. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** export annual effectiveness summary for Art. 32 evidence. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Security Testing Evidence Aggregation (Pen Test / Red Team)," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of GDPR control activity.

This search begins with `| inputlookup gdpr_security_test_register.csv` — the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `gdpr_security_test_register.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

• Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
• Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
• Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup gdpr_security_test_register.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true gdpr_security_test_register_audit.csv` — this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
• Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the full search range) is the detection trigger — when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.24: GDPR Security Testing Evidence Aggregation (Pen Test / Red T]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.24,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `cadence_days`, `days_since`, `last_test_date`, `processing_activity`, `status`, `system_name`, `test_date_epoch`, `test_type`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table cadence_days, days_since, last_test_date, processing_activity, status, system_name, test_date_epoch, test_type
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `gdpr_ropa_register.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.24: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`overdue tests`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Time trend (`tests by quarter`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`systems past cadence`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 4 — Distribution (`coverage by business unit`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of security testing evidence aggregation (pen test / red team) — so we can show we look after private information the way the privacy rules that apply to us require.
